name: Compiler Pipeline Architecture
summary: Decision framework for how many representations a compiler should keep and what each lowering boundary should preserve or discard. The pipeline usually moves from source-faithful syntax to semantic IR, explicit control flow, SSA or bytecode, then backend-specific forms.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A compiler pipeline is a sequence of information trade-offs. Early representations preserve source shape for diagnostics and tooling; middle representations make names, types, control flow, effects, and lifetimes explicit; late representations commit to VM or machine-level execution details.

A common shape is:

```text
source
  -> tokens
  -> CST
  -> AST / typed syntax facade
  -> HIR: resolved names, types, desugarings
  -> MIR: CFG, temporaries, explicit drops/effects
  -> SSA or bytecode
  -> backend IR / machine code / VM execution
```

The question is not whether every compiler needs every layer. The question is which analyses become simpler if a layer exists, and which clients suffer if a layer is skipped.

sections.title: Information boundaries
sections.content:
Each lowering boundary should be deliberate:

| Boundary | Gains | Loses or weakens |
|---|---|---|
| CST -> AST | Analysis-friendly nodes | Exact source trivia unless retained separately. |
| AST -> HIR | Resolved names, stable IDs, types | Some raw syntactic choices. |
| HIR -> MIR | Explicit CFG, temporaries, drops, effects | Source expression shape. |
| MIR -> SSA | Direct value-flow, optimization substrate | Some structured control and language-specific semantics. |
| MIR/SSA -> bytecode | Portable executable contract | Easy IR evolution; bytecode becomes ABI. |
| IR -> machine code | Peak execution path | Portability and inspectability. |

Diagnostics, debuggers, and refactoring tools often need side tables to recover information lost by lower layers. If those tables are not designed with the lowering, they are usually incomplete.

sections.title: Running example across IR layers
sections.content:
The corpus uses a small `abs` function as a running example across layers. Read these records in order to see the same computation move from source-shaped semantic IR to explicit control flow, SSA joins, and bytecode dispatch:

1. High-Level IR — source with resolved names and types.
2. Mid-Level IR — control-flow graph and block parameters.
3. SSA — φ-functions versus block parameters.
4. Bytecode — stack versus register encoding.

This kind of running example is valuable for compiler teams because it exposes exactly where information moves from syntax to semantics to execution.

sections.title: How many IRs should a compiler have?
sections.content:
A small compiler can begin with AST -> bytecode or AST -> backend IR. That is often the right bootstrap. Extra IR layers become worth it when they own a distinct responsibility:

- HIR is worth it when diagnostics, macros, lints, or language-server features need resolved but source-adjacent structure.
- MIR is worth it when control-flow-sensitive analyses matter: borrow checking, definite assignment, drop elaboration, effect ordering, async lowering, or pattern-match compilation.
- SSA is worth it when optimization and register allocation need direct value-flow.
- Bytecode is worth it when portability, introspection, sandboxing, or tiered execution matter.
- MLIR-style dialects are worth it when several domain-specific abstraction levels must coexist.

The anti-pattern is one universal IR that is simultaneously source-faithful, easy to type check, easy to optimize, easy to execute, and easy to debug. Production compilers generally stop trying to make one representation serve all clients.
